summary: Simon advocates for Microsoft OneNote as his go-to tool for organizing all aspects of life, from work projects to travel planning. He argues that OneNote's hierarchical structure of notebooks, sections, and pages allows notes to grow organically without losing organization. The post uses a two-month Asia trip as a practical example of how OneNote handles complex, evolving projects. He emphasizes that the tool's real power comes from long-term use, becoming a single repository for all essential information across work, personal life, and education.
key_points: OneNote's nested structure (notebooks, section groups, sections, pages) allows organic growth without losing organization
The 'write first, structure later' approach accommodates projects that evolve unpredictably
Full-text search including text in images makes finding information fast
Shared notebooks dramatically reduce coordination meetings and communication overhead
Cross-platform sync and offline access make it a true 'single source of truth'
money_quotes: Maybe the biggest feature itself is to organisation and structure inside OneNote
The real power comes with time, the more and the longer you use it, the more it gets to your one and only place to go for
This is really the beauty of OneNote, while most times you have no clue how the structure will look like in the end
You're having all essential information any kind of types in ONE single place
Shared every note together which reduced the amount of communication and coordination meetings to an absolute minimum
key_insight: The best note-taking system is one that lets you write first and restructure later, growing organically as your projects evolve.
